currentSnapshot.definition: Made or produced by human beings rather than occurring naturally; not genuine; simulated. In computing: produced by artificial intelligence.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: Carries both a neutral sense (artificial sweetener) and a pejorative one (artificial smile). The phrase 'artificial intelligence' (AI) has made the word newly prominent and complex.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: The artificial intelligence model generated a convincing but entirely fictional response.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Made with skill or art; the product of human craft and ingenuity. Artful, contrived by art.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: From Latin 'artificialis' (of or belonging to art). In early use, being 'artificial' was praiseworthy — it meant skilled, crafted, made with mastery.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The artificial beauty of the garden reflected the gardener's considerable art.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Produced by human work rather than natural processes; man-made as opposed to natural.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The contrast with 'natural' emerged as the dominant framework. No longer inherently positive — could be neutral or mildly pejorative depending on context.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: These artificial flowers, though cleverly made, lack the fragrance of nature.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Not genuine; simulated; assumed for effect; insincere.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: By the Romantic era, 'artificial' had acquired a strongly pejorative shade — 'artificial manners', 'artificial sentiment'. Contrast with the Romantic ideal of authentic nature.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: Her artificial smiles concealed a deep unhappiness.

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From 'made with skill' (positive) through 'man-made' (neutral) to 'simulated/fake' (negative or neutral depending on context).
summaryOfChange.longSummary: Artificial entered English with an entirely positive connotation: it meant made with artistry and skill, from Latin 'ars' (art). Over the Early Modern period it shifted to a neutral contrast with 'natural'. The Romantic reaction against industrialisation and artifice gave it a pejorative shade: artificial things were fake, insincere, inferior to nature. Today it occupies a complex middle ground — artificial flavours and artificial limbs are straightforward, while an 'artificial smile' is still mildly pejorative. The rise of 'artificial intelligence' has added a new neutral-to-positive connotation in tech contexts.

keyDates.label: First English use
keyDates.significance: Earliest recorded English use meaning 'made with art or skill'.

keyDates.label: Swift's 'Gulliver's Travels'
keyDates.significance: Swift satirises 'artificial' systems and reasoning, beginning to associate the word with the mechanical and soulless.

keyDates.label: Dartmouth Conference coins 'Artificial Intelligence'
keyDates.significance: John McCarthy's coinage of 'artificial intelligence' begins a new chapter in the word's history, giving it a powerful technical meaning.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – artificial, adj.
sources.excerpt: Made by art; contrived by human skill and labour. Later: not natural; simulated. Of persons: affected, not genuine.
sources.relevanceNote: Full arc from positive artisan skill to pejorative inauthenticity.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – artificial, adj. (modern)
sources.excerpt: Produced or introduced by human activity rather than occurring naturally. Also in 'artificial intelligence'.

sources.title: A Dictionary of the English Language – artificial
sources.author: Samuel Johnson
sources.excerpt: ARTIFICIAL. adj. Made by art; not natural; fictitious; not genuine; contrived with skill.
sources.relevanceNote: Johnson captures the transition point — both 'made with skill' and 'not genuine' coexist.

relatedWorks.title: Gulliver's Travels
relatedWorks.creator: Jonathan Swift
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: Swift satirises artificial systems of knowledge and government, associating 'artificial' with the mechanical, soulless, and absurd.

relatedPages.title: Pejoration
relatedPages.summary: How and why word meanings become more negative over time.

relatedConcepts.label: natural
relatedConcepts.note: The rise of 'natural' as a positive term is the mirror image of artificial's journey.

relatedConcepts.label: synthetic
relatedConcepts.note: Synthetic took over some of artificial's neutral sense in the 20th century.

ambiguityNotes: In contemporary use, 'artificial' is often neutral in technical contexts (artificial limb, artificial sweetener) but retains pejorative connotations in personal/social contexts (artificial smile, artificial personality).
The AI field's use of 'artificial intelligence' has begun to rehabilitate the word's connotations in some tech-forward communities.

timelineEvents.title: Enters English meaning 'made with art'
timelineEvents.summary: From Latin 'artificialis', the word initially praised skilled human craft.

timelineEvents.title: Johnson captures the turning point
timelineEvents.summary: Johnson's Dictionary shows 'artificial' meaning both 'made with skill' and 'fictitious/not genuine' — the positive and negative senses coexisting.

timelineEvents.title: Romanticism makes 'natural' the ideal
timelineEvents.summary: The Romantic reaction against industrialisation elevated 'natural' as the supreme value, making 'artificial' a term of critique for inauthenticity.

timelineEvents.title: Dartmouth: 'Artificial Intelligence' coined
timelineEvents.summary: John McCarthy coins 'artificial intelligence' at the Dartmouth Conference, beginning a new positive technical register for the word.
